topic: How we handle timezone complexity when payroll runs at midnight across GCC offices
format: Describe a technical problem that took longer than it should have — what made it hard, what fixed it, what you'd do differently
post:
Payroll ran at 12:00 AM. Three offices. Three timezones. One wrong assumption in our scheduler.

We stored "midnight" as a UTC timestamp and applied it uniformly across our GCC clients. Oman (UTC+4), Saudi (UTC+3), UAE (UTC+4 — but not always, DST edge cases exist). Simple math, right?

It wasn't.

The real problem wasn't the offset. WPS payroll Oman submissions have a hard bank cut-off tied to local midnight — not server time. One miscalculated run means a failed WPS file, a delayed salary transfer, and a compliance flag with the Ministry of Labour Oman. That's not a bug ticket. That's someone's salary arriving late.

What fixed it: we stopped storing time and started storing intent. Each tenant declares their payroll timezone explicitly. The scheduler resolves execution time per-tenant at runtime using Luxon — not at write time. MySQL stores UTC. The application layer owns the conversion. No assumptions baked in anywhere.

The tradeoff is real: more complexity per query, a heavier test surface. We decided it was worth it. We're at 4,000+ automated tests now, and timezone edge cases have their own suite.

What I'd do differently: model this on day one. Not after the first client goes live.

If you're building multi-tenant payroll for the GCC, "it's just an offset" will bite you. Usually at midnight.
